topic: A person finds out they were adopted through a DNA test
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope felt thin in my hand, the official seal stark against the cheap paper. I stared at the printed results, the highlighted percentages swimming in my vision. It was a genetic blueprint, a definitive answer to a question I hadn't even bothered to ask. My thumb traced the lines of my own DNA sequence, a complex pattern that suddenly seemed utterly meaningless. I placed the paper on the worn wooden desk, the sound barely registering over the hum of the refrigerator. There was no surge of shock, no sudden tidal wave of emotion; just a slow, steady settling. I picked up a pen, not to analyze, but just to mark the date. It was another piece of data, filed away.

[story 2]

When the doctor finally laid out the comparison charts, I kept my gaze fixed on the pattern of the fluorescent lights overhead. The information they presented was factual, a series of biological facts about my origins. I nodded once, a small, almost automatic movement, before looking down at my hands, flexing my fingers as if checking their structural integrity. The weight of the revelation didn't press down; it simply hung in the air, detached and distant. I managed a flat tone when I asked the follow-up questions, my voice level, devoid of inflection. It was just another piece of life history being cataloged.

[story 3]

I sat on the edge of the sofa, the silence of the apartment amplifying the quiet clicking of my watch. The DNA report lay open on my lap, a collection of markers and probabilities. I didn't feel anything significant. The information registered in the back of my mind, like a footnote in a long, unread book. I shifted my weight, pulling my knees up to my chest, a small, self-contained gesture of withdrawal. I glanced toward the window, where the city lights blurred into streaks of color. Whatever this meant, it didn't alter the immediate reality of the room, the temperature, the dull ache behind my eyes. It was just a fact, concluded.
